1: lĕkhû nĕrannĕnāh laYHWH — come, let us sing joyfully to the LORD. Rānan (to give a ringing shout of joy — the loud, exuberant communal cry that opens worship). The cohortative nĕrannĕnāh (let us shout/sing) is the liturgical call to corporate worship. nārîʿāh lĕṣûr yishʿênû — let us shout aloud to the Rock who saves us! Rûaʿ (hiphil: to shout in triumph/worship — the blast of joy). Ṣûr yishʿênû (Rock of our salvation) — the title from Ps 89:26 where the anointed king calls YHWH ṣûr yĕshûʿātî; here applied to the community's corporate salvation.
2: nĕqaddĕmāh phānāyw bĕtôdāh — let us come before him with thanksgiving. Qādam pānim (to come before the face — to approach the divine presence as a subject approaches a king). Tôdāh (thanksgiving — the specific sacrifice/act of thanksgiving in Israelite worship, from yādāh: to acknowledge, confess, thank). bizkĕmîrôt nārîaʿ lô — and celebrate him with songs of praise. Mĕzimmôt (psalms, songs — the musical compositions of praise offered in the temple). The two modes of approach: thanksgiving and song.
3: kî ʾēl gādôl YHWH ûmelek gādôl ʿal-kol-ʾĕlōhîm — for the LORD is a great God, a great King greater than all other gods. The grounding kî (for/because) gives the reason for worship: YHWH's divine identity. ʾĒl gādôl (great God) + melek gādôl (great King) — the double title acknowledges YHWH's divine nature and sovereign rule simultaneously. ʿAl-kol-ʾĕlōhîm (over all the gods) — the incomparability formula positioning YHWH over all other divine claimants, as in Ps 86:8; 89:6-7.
4: ʾăsher bĕyādô meḥqĕrê-ʾāreṣ — the deepest places of the earth are in his hand. Meḥqār (deep recesses, hidden depths — from ḥāqar: to search out) of the earth — the inaccessible underground depths. wĕtôʿăphôt hārîm lô — and the mountain peaks belong to him. Tôʿāphôt (heights, peaks — the extreme high points) of the mountains. The vertical sovereignty: from the deepest underground (meḥqĕrê-ʾāreṣ) to the highest peaks (tôʿăphôt hārîm) — the full vertical range of the created order belongs to YHWH.
5: ʾăsher-lô hayyām wĕhûʾ ʿāśāhû — the sea is his — he made it. Ownership grounded in creation: lô (his) because ʿāśāhû (he made it). The sea that the chaos-waters of Ps 93:3-4 threatened to overwhelm YHWH's rule belongs to him as its maker — possession precedes and defeats the chaos-threat. wĕyabbāshet yādāyw yāṣārû — his own hands shaped the dry land. Yāṣar (to form, shape — the potter's forming; cf. Gen 2:7 where YHWH forms the man from dust). The dry land formed by YHWH's hands — the same hands that hold the depths (v4). All the earth is his handiwork.
6: bōʾû nishṭaḥăweh wĕnikhrāʿāh — come, let us bow down and worship. The second call to worship (bōʾû: come — cf. v1's lĕkhû: come), now shifting from joyful shouting (vv1-2) to reverent prostration (shāḥāh: to bow low, prostrate oneself in worship) and kneeling (kāraʿ: to kneel). nibrĕkhāh liphnê-YHWH ʿōśênû — let us kneel before the LORD who made us. ʿĒśânû (who made us — from ʿāśāh: the same verb as v5 where YHWH made the sea). The congregation as YHWH's handiwork alongside the sea and dry land.
7: kî hûʾ ʾĕlōhênû waʾănaḥnû ʿam marʿîtô wĕṣōʾn yādô — for he is our God, and we are the people of his pasture, the flock he tends. The covenant identity: ʾĕlōhênû (our God) + ʿammô (his people) in the classical covenant formula. Ṣōʾn yādô (the flock of his hand) — the sheep held in God's hand, cared for with his own hand (cf. Ezek 34:31's good shepherd promise). hayyôm ʾim-bĕqōlô tishmaʿû — today — if only you would hear his voice. Hayyôm (today — the urgent, unrepeatable present moment that the divine voice occupies). The psalm pivots abruptly from worship-invitation to covenant-warning. The ʾim (if) marks the conditional threshold.
8: ʾal-taqshû lĕbabĕkhem kimrîbāh — do not harden your hearts, as at Meribah. Qāshāh (hiphil: to make hard, to harden — the deliberate stiffening of the heart against the divine voice). Mĕrîbāh (Meribah — the place of quarreling, from rîb: to contend; Exod 17:7; Num 20:13 where the people contended with God over water). kĕyôm massāh bammidebār — as on that day at Massah in the wilderness. Massāh (testing — from nāsāh: to test; Exod 17:7 where the location was named both Massah and Meribah). The wilderness generation as the paradigmatic warning against hardening.
9: ʾăsher nissûnî ʾăbôtêkhem — your ancestors put me to the test. Nāsāh (to test, to prove — the dangerous reversal: the creature testing the Creator). bĕḥānûnî gam-rāʾû phāʿălî — and challenged me, even after seeing all I had done. Bāḥan (to test, examine — a second verb for testing, as if the first were insufficient to express the audacity). The aggravating circumstance: gam-rāʾû phāʿălî (they also saw my works) — the testing happened despite (gam: also, even) the visual evidence of YHWH's miraculous deeds. Testing after seeing is the supreme ingratitude.
10: ʾarbaʿîm shānāh ʾāqûṭ bĕdôr — for forty years I was provoked by that generation. Qûṭ (qal: to loathe, be disgusted with — from a root related to deep revulsion; used here of God's attitude toward the wilderness generation). This is an unusually intense verb for divine reaction — not simply anger but something closer to moral nausea at persistent rebellion. wāʾōmar ʿam tōʿê-lēbāb hēm — and declared, 'This people's heart keeps wandering'. Tāʿāh (to wander, err, stray — as a sheep straying from the path; cf. Ps 119:176). The diagnosis: erring heart (tōʿê-lēbāb) — not occasional failure but a structural disposition to wander. wĕhēm lōʾ-yādĕʿû dĕrākhāy — they have never learned my ways. Yādaʿ (to know — covenant-knowing that includes relational fidelity). They have not internalized the divine ways despite forty years of experience with them.
11: ʾăsher-nishbaʿtî bĕʾappî — so in my anger I swore. Nishbaʿ (niphal of shābaʿ: to swear an oath) bĕʾappî (in my anger — the oath sworn in the moment of divine wrath). ʾim-yĕbōʾûn ʾel-mĕnûḥātî — 'They will never enter my rest.' Mĕnûḥāh (rest, resting-place — from nûaḥ: to rest, settle; the concept covers both the physical land-rest of Canaan, Gen 2:2's divine Sabbath rest, and the eschatological rest toward which both point). The ʾim before the oath is the negative oath-formula in Hebrew: ʾim yĕbōʾûn = they shall certainly not enter. The epistle to the Hebrews (Heb 3:7-4:11) reads this verse as describing a rest that remains available — the wilderness generation's exclusion does not exhaust the promise of rest; the hayyôm of v7 is still the door to enter it.
